name: AB_gU
long_name: U momentum tendency from Adams-Bashforth
comments_1: Component of the u momentum tendency term, TOTUTEND = Um_Cori + (Um_Advec - Um_Cori) + Um_Diss + Um_Ext + Um_dPhiX + AB_gU + Um_ImplD.
comments_2: Note: the model +x direction does not necessarily correspond to the geographical east-west direction because the x and y axes of the model's curvilinear lat-lon-cap (llc) grid have arbitrary orientations which vary within and across tiles.

name: AB_gV
long_name: V momentum tendency from Adams-Bashforth
comments_1: Component of the v momentum tendency term, TOTVTEND = Vm_Cori + (Vm_Advec - Vm_Cori) + Vm_Diss + Vm_Ext + Vm_dPhiY + AB_gV + Vm_ImplD.
comments_2: Note: the model +y direction does not necessarily correspond to the geographical north-south direction because the x and y axes of the model's curvilinear lat-lon-cap (llc) grid have arbitrary orientations which vary within and across tiles.

name: ADVr_SLT
long_name: Vertical advective flux of salinity
comments_1: Vertical advective flux of salinity (SALT) in the model +z direction through the top 'w' face of the tracer cell.
comments_2: Note: in the Arakawa-C grid, vertical flux quantities are staggered relative to the tracer cells faces. Positive ADVr_SLT(i,j,k_l) corresponds to +z upward fluxes through the top 'w' face of the tracer cell at (i,j,k). Salinity defined using CF convention 'Sea water salinity is the salt content of sea water, often on the Practical Salinity Scale of 1978. However, the unqualified term 'salinity' is generic and does not necessarily imply any particular method of calculation. The units of salinity are dimensionless and the units attribute should normally be given as 1e-3 or 0.001 i.e. parts per thousand.' see https://cfconventions.org/Data/cf-standard-names/73/build/cf-standard-name-table.html
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > SALINITY/DENSITY > SALT TRANSPORT

name: atmPload
long_name: Atmosphere surface pressure anomaly relative to a reference pressure of 101325 Pa

comments_2: Not adjusted by the ocean state estimation.
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN PRESSURE > SEA LEVEL PRESSURE

name: botTauX
long_name: bottom stress in the model +x direction
comments_1: Not required to close the momentum budget but provided for users interested in diagnosing bottom stress. Component of the bottom drag contribution to the u momentum explicit dissipation term, UBotDrag.
comments_2: Note: the model +x direction does not necessarily correspond to the geographical east-west direction because the x and y axes of the model's curvilinear lat-lon-cap (llc) grid have arbitrary orientations which vary within and across tiles.

name: botTauY
long_name: bottom stress in the model +y direction

name: DFrE_SLT
long_name: Vertical diffusive flux of salinity (explicit term)
comments_1: The explicit term of the vertical diffusive flux of salinity (SALT) in the model +z direction through the top 'w' face of the tracer cell. In this model an implicit scheme is used to calculate vertical diffusive tracer fluxes due to background diffusivity and the Kwz component of the GM-Redi tensor (vertical flux as a function of vertical gradient) while an explicit scheme is used to calculate the vertical diffusive fluxes from the Kwx and Kwy components of the GM-Redi tensor (vertical flux as a function of horizontal gradient). Both implicit and explicit components of vertical diffusive flux of salinity are provided.
comments_2: Note: in the Arakawa-C grid, vertical flux quantities are staggered relative to the tracer cells. Positive DFrE_SLT(i,j,k_l) corresponds to upward +z fluxes through the top 'w' face of the tracer cell at (i,j,k). Salinity defined using CF convention 'Sea water salinity is the salt content of sea water, often on the Practical Salinity Scale of 1978. However, the unqualified term 'salinity' is generic and does not necessarily imply any particular method of calculation. The units of salinity are dimensionless and the units attribute should normally be given as 1e-3 or 0.001 i.e. parts per thousand.' see https://cfconventions.org/Data/cf-standard-names/73/build/cf-standard-name-table.html
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > SALINITY/DENSITY > SALT TRANSPORT

long_name: Vertical diffusive flux of potential temperature (explicit term)
comments_1: The explicit term of the vertical diffusive flux of potential temperature (THETA) in the model +z direction through the top 'w' face of the tracer cell. In this model, an implicit scheme is used to calculate vertical diffusive tracer fluxes due to background diffusivity and the Kwz component of the GM-Redi tensor (vertical flux as a function of vertical gradient) while an explicit scheme is used to calculate the vertical diffusive fluxes from the Kwx and Kwy components of the GM-Redi tensor (vertical flux as a function of horizontal gradient). name: DIFFKR
long_name: Vertical diffusivity
comments_1: Background vertical diffusion coefficient for temperature and salinity. Total vertical diffusivity includes background diffusivity plus contributions from the GGL90 vertical mixing and the Gent-McWilliams/Redi parameterizations.
comments_2: Note: DIFFKR is a model control variable and has been optimized from a spatially-invariant first-guess value of 1e-5 m2 s-1.

name: DRHODR
long_name: Density stratification
comments_1: Density stratification: d(sigma) d z-1
comments_2: Note: density computations are done with in-situ density. The vertical derivatives of in-situ density and locally-referenced potential density are identical  The equation of state is a modified UNESCO formula by Jackett and McDougall (1995), which uses the model variable potential temperature as input assuming a horizontally and temporally constant pressure of $p_0=-g ho_{0} z$.
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > SALINITY/DENSITY, EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > SALINITY/DENSITY > DENSITY
internal note: is stratification calculated on z* or z coordinates?

name: ETAN
long_name: Model sea level anomaly
comments_1: Model sea level anomaly WITHOUT corrections for global mean density (steric) changes, inverted barometer effect, or volume displacement due to submerged sea-ice and snow.
comments_2: Note: ETAN should NOT be used for comparisons with altimetry data products because ETAN is NOT corrected for (a) global mean steric sea level changes related to density changes in the Boussinesq volume-conserving model (Greatbatch correction, see sterGloH) nor (b) sea level displacement due to submerged sea-ice and snow (see sIceLoad). These corrections ARE made for the variables SSH and SSHNOIBC.
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > SEA SURFACE TOPOGRAPHY > SEA SURFACE HEIGHT

name: EXFaqh
long_name: Atmosphere surface (2 m) specific humidity
comments_1: Surface (2 m) specific humidity over open water
comments_2: Note: sum of ERA-Interim surface specific humidity and the control adjustment from ocean state estimation.
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > ATMOSPHERE > ATMOSPHERIC WATER VAPOR > WATER VAPOR INDICATORS > HUMIDITY > SPECIFIC HUMIDITY

name: EXFatemp
long_name: Atmosphere surface (2 m) air temperature
comments_1: Surface (2 m) air temperature over open water
comments_2: Note: sum of ERA-Interim surface air temperature and the control adjustment from ocean state estimation.
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > ATMOSPHERE > ATMOSPHERIC TEMPERATURE > SURFACE TEMPERATURE > AIR TEMPERATURE

name: EXFempmr
long_name: Open ocean net surface freshwater flux from precipitation, evaporation, and runoff
comments_1: Net surface freshwater flux from precipitation, evaporation, and runoff per unit area in open water (not covered by sea-ice). Excludes freshwater fluxes involving sea-ice and snow.
comments_2: Note: calculated as EXFevap-EXFpreci-EXFroff.
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN CIRCULATION > FRESH WATER FLUX

name: EXFevap
long_name: Open ocean evaporation rate
comments_1: Evaporation rate per unit area of open water (not covered by sea-ice).
comments_2: Note: calculated using the bulk formula following Large and Yeager (2004) NCAR/TN-460+STR.
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > ATMOSPHERE > ATMOSPHERIC WATER VAPOR > WATER VAPOR PROCESSES > EVAPORATION

name: EXFhl
long_name: Open ocean air-sea latent heat flux
comments_1: Air-sea latent heat flux per unit area of open water (not covered by sea-ice).
comments_2: Note: calculated from the bulk formula following Large and Yeager (2004) NCAR/TN-460+STR.
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN HEAT BUDGET > EVAPORATION
internal note: negative almost everywhere (heat loss) because evaporation cools the ocean

name: EXFhs
long_name: Open ocean air-sea sensible heat flux
comments_1: Air-sea sensible heat flux per unit area of open water (not covered by sea-ice).
comments_2: Note: calculated from the bulk formula following Large and Yeager (2004) NCAR/TN-460+STR.
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN HEAT BUDGET > HEAT FLUX
internal note: negative almost everywhere in winter hemisphere (heat loss) because sensible heat flux cools the ocean

name: EXFlwdn
long_name: Downward longwave radiative flux
comments_1: Downward longwave radiative flux
comments_2: Note: sum of ERA-Interim downward longwave radiation and the control adjustment from ocean state estimation.
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN HEAT BUDGET > LONGWAVE RADIATION
internal note: positive everywhere because longwave radiation warms the ocean

name: EXFlwnet
long_name: Net open ocean longwave radiative flux
comments_1: Net longwave radiative flux per unit area of open water (not covered by sea-ice).
comments_2: Note: net longwave radiation over open water calculated from downward longwave radiation (EXFlwdn) and upward longwave radiation from ocean and sea-ice thermal emission (Stefan-Boltzman law).
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN HEAT BUDGET > LONGWAVE RADIATION
internal note: generally positive because the net longwave radiation represents a 30-60 W m-2 ocean heat loss

name: EXFpreci
long_name: Precipitation rate
comments_1: Precipitation rate
comments_2: Note: sum of ERA-Interim precipitation and the control adjustment from ocean state estimation.
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > ATMOSPHERE > PRECIPITATION > PRECIPITATION RATE > SURFACE PRECIPITATION

name: EXFpress
long_name: Atmosphere surface pressure
comments_1: Atmospheric pressure field at sea level
comments_2: Note: ERA-Interim atmospheric pressure, with air tides removed using a variety of methods. Not adjusted by the ocean state estimation.
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN PRESSURE > SEA LEVEL PRESSURE

name: EXFqnet
long_name: Open ocean net air-sea heat flux
comments_1: Net air-sea heat flux (turbulent and radiative) per unit area of open water (not covered by sea-ice).
comments_2: Note: net upward heat flux over open water, calculated as EXFlwnet + EXFswnet - EXFhl - EXFhs.
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN HEAT BUDGET > HEAT FLUX

name: EXFroff
long_name: River runoff
comments_1: River runoff freshwater flux
comments_2: Note: not adjusted by the optimization.
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > TERRESTRIAL HYDROSPHERE > SURFACE WATER > SURFACE WATER PROCESSES/MEASUREMENTS > RUNOFF > RUNOFF RATE

name: EXFswdn
long_name: Downwelling shortwave radiative flux
comments_1: Downward shortwave radiative flux
comments_2: Note: sum of ERA-Interim downward shortwave radiation and the control adjustment from ocean state estimation.
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN HEAT BUDGET > SHORTWAVE RADIATION
internal note: positive almost everywhere because shortwave radiation warms the ocean.  In some high-latitude regions in winter, EXFswdn can be negative due to the control adjustment from ocean state estimation.

name: EXFswnet
long_name: Open ocean net shortwave radiative flux
comments_1: Net shortwave radiative flux per unit area of open water (where there is no sea-ice).
comments_2: Note: net shortwave radiation over open water calculated from downward shortwave flux (EXFswdn) and ocean surface albdeo.
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN HEAT BUDGET > SHORTWAVE RADIATION
internal note: negative almost everywhere because shortwave radiation warms the ocean. In some high-latitude regions in winter, EXFswnet can be positive due to the control adjustment from ocean state estimation.

name: EXFtaux
long_name: Wind stress in the model +x direction
comments_1: Wind stress in the model +x direction at the tracer cell center.
comments_2: Note: EXFtaux is the stress applied to the ice-free ocean surface and sea-ice covered surface. When sea-ice is present, the total stress applied to the ocean surface in the +x direction is NOT EXFtaux, but a combination of EXFtaux wind stress in the open water fraction and a stress from sea-ice in the ice-covered fraction (see oceTAUX).
direction: >0 increases horizontal velocity in the +x direction (UVEL)
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN WINDS > WIND STRESS

name: EXFtauy
long_name: Wind stress in the model +y direction
comments_1: Wind stress in the model +y direction at the tracer cell center.
comments_2: Note: EXFtauy is the stress applied to the ice-free ocean surface and sea-ice covered surface. When sea-ice is present, the total stress applied to the ocean surface in the +y direction is NOT EXFtauy, but a combination of EXFtauy wind stress in the open water fraction and a stress from sea-ice in the ice-covered fraction (see oceTAUY).
direction: >0 increases horizontal velocity in the +y direction (VVEL)
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN WINDS > WIND STRESS

name: EXFuwind
long_name: Wind speed at 10m in the model +x direction
comments_1: Wind speed at 10m in the model +x direction at the tracer cell center
comments_2: Note: this model is forced with wind stress (see EXFtaux) not vector winds converted to wind stress using bulk formulae. EXFuwind is calculated by converting wind stress to vector wind using bulk formulae.
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN WINDS > SURFACE WINDS

name: EXFvwind
long_name: Wind speed at 10m in the model +y direction
comments_1: Wind speed at 10m in the model +y direction at the tracer cell center
comments_2: Note: this model is forced with wind stress (see EXFtauy) not vector winds converted to wind stress using bulk formulae. EXFvwind is calculated by converting wind stress to vector wind using bulk formulae.
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN WINDS > SURFACE WINDS

name: EXFwspee
long_name: Wind speed
comments_1: Wind speed total magnitude at 10m. Only used for the calculation of air-sea fluxes using bulk formulae, not used for stress forcing of the ocean model.
comments_2: Note: this variable is not adjusted during the state estimation and therefore may not be consistent with the vector wind speed terms EXFuwind and EXFvwind which are calculated from the adjusted EXFtaux and EXFtauy terms. EXFwspee != sqrt(EXFuwind**2 + EXFvwind**2).
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN WINDS > SURFACE WINDS, EARTH SCIENCE > ATMOSPHERE > ATMOSPHERIC WINDS > SURFACE WINDS > WIND SPEED

name: GM_PsiX
long_name: Gent-McWilliams bolus transport streamfunction in the model +x direction
comments_1: Gent-McWilliams bolus transport streamfunction 'u' component.
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN CIRCULATION

name: GM_PsiY
long_name: Gent-McWilliams bolus transport streamfunction in the model +y direction
comments_1: Gent-McWilliams bolus transport streamfunction 'v' component.
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN CIRCULATION

name: ICFfwFlx
long_name: Ice front freshwater flux
comments_1: This freshwater flux term is provided with respect the horizontal area of the grid cell (rA), even though the flux occurs across the vertical face of the grid cell adjacent to the ice shelf front. To calculate the total freshwater flux due to ocean-ice sheet interaction in a model grid cell, sum the ICFfwFlx and SHIfwFlx terms.
comments_2: This diagnostic is only nonzero for grid cells adjacent to ice shelf fronts.

name: ICFhtFlx
long_name: Ice front heat flux
comments_1: This heat flux term is provided with respect the horizontal area of the grid cell (rA), even though the flux occurs across the vertical face of the grid cell adjacent to the ice shelf front. To calculate the total heat flux due to ocean-ice sheet interaction in a model grid cell, sum the ICFhtFlx and SHIhtFlx terms.
comments_2: This diagnostic is only nonzero for grid cells adjacent to ice shelf fronts.

name: KAPGM
long_name: Gent-McWilliams diffusivity
comments_1: Gent-McWilliams diffusivity coefficient as described in Gent and McWilliams (1990, JPO).
comments_2: Note: KAPGM is a model control variable and has been optimized from a spatially invariant first guess of 1e3 m2 s-1.

name: KAPREDI
long_name: Along-isopycnal diffusivity
comments_1: Redi along-isopycnal diffusivity coefficient as described in Redi (1982, JPO).
comments_2: Note: KAPREDI is a model control variable and has been optimized from a spatially invariant first guess of 1e3 m2 s-1.

name: momHDiv
long_name: divergence of horizontal velocities
comments_1: Divergence of the horizontal velocities calculated as hDiv(i,j) = 1/rA(i,j) * ( U(i_g+1,j) dyG(i_g+1,j) - U(i_g,j) dyG(i_g,j) ) + ( V(i,j_g+1) dxG (i,j_g+1) - V(i,j_g) dxG(i,j_g) ).
comments_2: Note this calculates divergence using hDivScheme=2 which may have larger errors at face corners (see mom_calc_hdiv.F).

name: momKE
long_name: kinetic energy associated with horizontal velocities
comments_1: Kinetic energy of the ocean calculated as momKE(i,j) = 0.25 * (U(i_g,j)**2 + U(i_g+1,j)**2 + V(i,j_g)**2 + V(i,j_g+1)**2).
comments_2: MITgcm KEscheme=0 used here calculates kinetic energy at tracer cell centers by averaging the kinetic energy contributions from the four surrounding velocity points. Alternative formulations are possible (see mom_calc_ke.F).

name: momVort3
long_name: vertical component of relative vorticity
comments_1: The vertical component of relative vorticity is calculated as momVort3(i_g,j_g) = ( V(i,j_g) dyC(i,j_g) - V(i-1,j_g) dyC(i-1,j_g) - U(i_g,j) dxC(i_g,j) + U(i_g,j-1) dxC(i_g,j-1) ) / rAz(i_g,j_g).
comments_2: momVort3 is naturally located at grid cell corners (i_g, j_g). At face corners, the calculation is handled differently (see mom_calc_relvort3.F for details).

name: MXLDEPTH
long_name: Mixed-layer depth diagnosed using the density criterion of Kara et al., 2000
comments_1: Depth level where potential density exceeds the surface potential density + delta rho, with delta rho = alpha(surface layer) * 0.8 degrees Celsius. See Kara et al. (JGR, 2000).
comments_2: Note: the Kara et al. (2000) criterion is not appropriate for all applications. Users should calculate their own mixed layer depths as needed using vertical stratification (DRHODR) and/or density anomaly (RHOAnoma).
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN CIRCULATION > OCEAN MIXED LAYER

name: OBP
long_name: Ocean bottom pressure provided as equivalent water thickness
comments_1: OBP excludes the contribution from global mean atmospheric pressure and is therefore suitable for comparisons with GRACE data products.
comments_2: OBP is calculated as follows. First, calculate ocean hydrostatic bottom pressure anomaly, PHIBOT; PHIBOT = $p_b/rhoConst - g H$, where $p_b$ is ocean hydrostatic bottom pressure, $rhoConst$ is a reference density (1029 kg m-3), $g$ is acceleration due to gravity (9.81 m s-2), and $H$ is a reference ocean depth, the distance from the liquid ocean surface at rest to the seafloor. $p_b$ is the vertical integral of $g ho dz^*$, where $ho$ is density (density anomaly + reference density) and $dz^{*}$ is the time-varying grid cell thickness. Then, OBP = PHIBOT/g + C, where C is the sum of two corrections: 1) global mean steric sea level changes related to density changes in the Boussinesq volume-conserving model (Greatbatch correction, see sterGloH) and 2) global mean atmospheric pressure variations. Use OBP for comparisons with ocean bottom pressure data products that have been corrected for global mean atmospheric pressure variations. GRACE data typically ARE corrected for global mean atmospheric pressure variations. In contrast, ocean bottom pressure gauge data typically ARE NOT corrected for global mean atmospheric pressure variations.
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN PRESSURE, EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN PRESSURE > WATER PRESSURE

name: OBPAnoma
long_name: Ocean bottom pressure anomaly provided as equivalent water thickness
comments_1: OBPAnoma is calculated as OBP minus the 2005-2010 mean of OBP at each grid cell. See OBP for details.
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN PRESSURE, EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN PRESSURE > WATER PRESSURE

name: OBPGMAP
long_name: Ocean bottom pressure provided as equivalent water thickness, includes global mean atmospheric pressure
comments_1: OBPGMAP includes the contribution from global mean atmospheric pressure and is therefore suitable for comparisons with ocean bottom pressure gauge data products.
comments_2: OBPGMAP is calculated as follows. First, we calculate ocean hydrostatic bottom pressure anomaly, PHIBOT; PHIBOT = $p_b/rhoConst - g H$, where $p_b$ = model ocean hydrostatic bottom pressure, rhoConst = reference density (1029 kg m-3), $g$ is acceleration due to gravity (9.81 m s-2), and $H$ is a reference ocean depth, the distance from the liquid ocean surface at rest to the seafloor. $p_b$ is the vertical integral of $g ho dz^{*}$, where $ho$ is density (density anomaly + reference density) and $dz^{*}$ is the time-varying grid cell thickness. Then, OBPGMAP = PHIBOT/g + a correction for global mean steric sea level changes related to density changes in the Boussinesq volume-conserving model (Greatbatch correction, see sterGloH). Use OBPGMAP for comparisons with ocean bottom pressure data products that have NOT been corrected for global mean atmospheric pressure variations. GRACE data typically ARE corrected for global mean atmospheric pressure variations. In contrast, ocean bottom pressure gauge data typically ARE NOT corrected for global mean atmospheric pressure variations.
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN PRESSURE, EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN PRESSURE > WATER PRESSURE

name: OBPGMAPA
long_name: Ocean bottom pressure anomaly provided as equivalent water thickness, includes global mean atmospheric pressure
comments_1: OBPGMAPA is calculated as OBPGMAP minus the 2005-2010 mean of OBP at each grid cell. See OBPGMAP for details.
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN PRESSURE, EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN PRESSURE > WATER PRESSURE

name: oceFWflx
long_name: Net freshwater flux into the ocean
comments_1: Net freshwater flux into the ocean including contributions from runoff, evaporation, precipitation, and mass exchange with sea-ice due to melting and freezing and snow melting.
comments_2: Note: oceFWflx does NOT include freshwater fluxes between the atmosphere and sea-ice and snow. The variable 'SIatmFW' accounts for freshwater fluxes out of the combined ocean+sea-ice+snow reservoir.
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN CIRCULATION > FRESH WATER FLUX

name: oceQnet
long_name: Net heat flux into the ocean surface
comments_1: Net heat flux into the ocean surface from all processes: air-sea turbulent and radiative fluxes and turbulent and conductive fluxes between the ocean and sea-ice and snow.
comments_2: Note: oceQnet does not include the change in ocean heat content due to changing ocean mass (oceFWflx). Mass fluxes from evaporation, precipitation, and runoff (EXFempmr) happen at the same temperature as the ocean surface temperature. Consequently, EmPmR does not change ocean surface temperature. Conversely, mass fluxes due to sea-ice thickening/thinning and snow melt in the model are assumed to happen at a fixed temperature of 0 degrees Celsius. Consequently, mass fluxes due to phase changes between seawater and sea-ice and snow induce a heat flux when the ocean surface temperature is not 0 degrees Celsius. The variable TFLUX does include the change in ocean heat content due to changing ocean mass.
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN HEAT BUDGET > HEAT FLUX
internal note: positive in summer hemisphere (net heat gain)

name: oceQsw
long_name: Net shortwave radiative flux across the ocean surface
comments_1: Net shortwave radiative flux across the ocean surface.
comments_2: Note: Shortwave radiation penetrates below the surface grid cell.
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN HEAT BUDGET > HEAT FLUX, EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN HEAT BUDGET > SHORTWAVE RADIATION
internal note: positive in summer hemisphere (net heat gain)

name: oceSflux
long_name: Net salt flux into uppermost ocean grid cell
comments_1: Nonzero where salt is exchanged between the ocean and sea-ice during melting and freezing.
comments_2: Note: oceSflux does NOT include salt fluxes into the ocean below the uppermost grid cell (k > 1). See oceSPflx for salt fluxes due to brine rejection.

name: oceSPDep
long_name: Salt plume depth
comments_1: Depth of parameterized salt plumes formed due to brine rejection during sea-ice formation.

name: oceSPflx
long_name: Net salt flux into the ocean due to brine rejection
comments_1: Net salt flux into the ocean due to brine rejection during sea-ice formation.
comments_2: Note: units are grams of salt per square meter per second, not salinity per square meter per second.
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > SALINITY/DENSITY > SALT TRANSPORT

name: oceSPtnd
long_name: Salt tendency due to the vertical transport of salt in high-salinity brine plumes
comments_1: Note: units are grams of salt per square meter per second, not salinity per square meter per second.
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > SALINITY/DENSITY > SALT TRANSPORT

name: oceTAUX
long_name: Ocean surface stress in the model +x direction
comments_1: Ocean surface stress due to wind and sea-ice in the +x direction centered over the 'u' side of the the native model grid.
comments_2: Note: in the Arakawa-C grid, wind stress acts on horizontal velocities which are staggered relative to the tracer cells. Positive oceTAUX(i_g,j) corresponds to momentum fluxes in the +x direction at 'u' edge of the surface tracer cell at. Note, the model +x direction does not necessarily correspond to the geographical east-west direction because the x and y axes of the model's curvilinear lat-lon-cap (llc) grid have arbitrary orientations which vary within and across tiles.
direction: >0 increases horizontal velocity in the +x direction (UVEL)
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN WINDS > WIND STRESS

name: oceTAUY
long_name: Ocean surface stress in the model +y direction
comments_1: Ocean surface stress due to wind and sea-ice in the +y direction centered over the 'v' side of the the native model grid.
comments_2: Note: in the Arakawa-C grid, wind stress acts on horizontal velocities which are staggered relative to the tracer cells. Positive oceTAUY(i_g,j) corresponds to momentum fluxes in the +y direction at 'v' edge of the surface tracer cell. Note, the model +y direction does not necessarily correspond to the geographical north-south direction because the x and y axes of the model's curvilinear lat-lon-cap (llc) grid have arbitrary orientations which vary within and across tiles.
direction: >0 increases horizontal velocity in the +y direction (VVEL)
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN WINDS > WIND STRESS

name: PHIBOT
long_name: Ocean hydrostatic bottom pressure anomaly
comments_1: PHIBOT = $p_b/rhoConst - g H$, where $p_b$ is the hydrostatic ocean bottom pressure, $rhoConst$ is a reference density (1029 kg m-3), $g$ is acceleration due to gravity (9.81 m s-2), and $H$ is a reference ocean depth, the distance from the liquid ocean surface at rest to the seafloor. $p_b$ is the vertical integral of $g ho dz^{*}$, where $ho$ is density (density anomaly + reference density) and $dz^*$ is the time-varying grid cell thickness. Units: $p_b$:[kg m-1 s-2], $rhoConst$:[kg m-3], $g$:[m s-2], $H$:[m].
comments_2: Note: includes atmospheric pressure loading. PHIBOT accounts for the model's time-varying grid cell thickness (z* coordinate system). PHIBOT is NOT corrected for global mean steric sea level changes related to density changes in the Boussinesq volume-conserving model (Greatbatch correction, see sterGloH), and therefore should NOT be used for comparisons with ocean bottom pressure data. Instead, see OBPGMAP and OBP.
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN PRESSURE, EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN PRESSURE > WATER PRESSURE

name: PHIHYD
long_name: Ocean hydrostatic pressure anomaly
comments_1: PHIHYD = $p(k)/rhoConst - g z^*(k,t)$, where $p(k)$ is the hydrostatic ocean pressure at depth level $k$, $rhoConst$ is a reference density (1029 kg m-3), $g$ is acceleration due to gravity (9.81 m s-2), and $z^*(k,t)$ is model depth at level $k$ and time $t$. Units: $p$:[kg m-1 s-2], $rhoConst$:[kg m-3], $g$:[m s-2], $z^*$:[m].
comments_2: Note: includes atmospheric pressure loading. Quantity referred to in some contexts as hydrostatic pressure anomaly. PHIBOT accounts for the model's time-varying grid cell thickness (z* coordinate system). See PHIHYDcR for hydrostatic pressure potential anomaly calculated using time-invariant grid cell thicknesses. PHIHYD is NOT corrected for global mean steric sea level changes related to density changes in the Boussinesq volume-conserving model (Greatbatch correction, see sterGloH).
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN PRESSURE, EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN PRESSURE > WATER PRESSURE

name: PHIHYDcR
long_name: Ocean hydrostatic pressure anomaly at constant depths
comments_1: PHIHYD = $p(k)/rhoConst - g z(k)$, where $p$ is the hydrostatic ocean pressure at depth level $k$, $rhoConst$ is a reference density (1029 kg m-3), $g$ is acceleration due to gravity (9.81 m s-2), and $z(k)$ is reference model depth at level $k$. Note, PHIHYDcR intentionally does not account for time-variations of model grid cell thickness associated with the $z^*$ coordinate system. Units: $p$:[kg m-1 s-2], $rhoConst$:[kg m-3], $g$:[m s-2], $z$:[m].
comments_2: Note: includes atmospheric pressure loading. Quantity referred to in some contexts as hydrostatic pressure potential anomaly. PHIHYDcR is calculated with respect to the model's initial, time-invariant grid cell thicknesses. See PHIHYD for hydrostatic pressure anomaly calculated using model's time-variable grid cell thicknesses ($z^*$ coordinate system). PHIHYDcR is is NOT corrected for global mean steric sea level changes related to density changes in the Boussinesq volume-conserving model (Greatbatch correction, see sterGloH).
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN PRESSURE, EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN PRESSURE > WATER PRESSURE

name: RHOAnoma
long_name: in-situ seawater density anomaly
comments_1: in-situ seawater density anomaly relative to the reference density, rhoConst. rhoConst = 1029 kg m-3.
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > SALINITY/DENSITY, EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > SALINITY/DENSITY > DENSITY
internal note: INCLUDE HFACC FOR VOLUME MEAN CALCULATIONS

name: SALT
long_name: Salinity
comments_1: Defined using CF convention 'Sea water salinity is the salt content of sea water, often on the Practical Salinity Scale of 1978. However, the unqualified term 'salinity' is generic and does not necessarily imply any particular method of calculation.
comments_2: The units of salinity are dimensionless and the units attribute should normally be given as 1e-3 or 0.001 i.e. parts per thousand.' see https://cfconventions.org/Data/cf-standard-names/73/build/cf-standard-name-table.html
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > SALINITY/DENSITY > SALINITY
internal note: INCLUDE HFACC FOR VOLUME MEAN CALCULATIONS

name: SFLUX
long_name: Rate of change of total ocean salinity per m2 accounting for mass fluxes
comments_1: The rate of change of total ocean salinity due to freshwater fluxes across the liquid surface and the addition or removal of mass.
comments_2: Note: the global area integral of SFLUX matches the time-derivative of total ocean salinity (psu s-1). Unlike oceFWflx, SFLUX includes the contribution to the total ocean salinity from changing ocean mass (e.g. from the addition or removal of freshwater in oceFWflx).

name: SHIfwFlx
long_name: Ice-shelf freshwater flux
comments_1: This freshwater flux term is provided with respect the horizontal area of the grid cell (rA). To calculate the total freshwater flux due to ocean-ice sheet interaction in a model grid cell, sum the ICFfwFlx and SHIfwFlx terms.
comments_2: This diagnostic is only nonzero for grid cells below ice shelves.

name: SHIhtFlx
long_name: Ice-shelf heat flux
comments_1: This heat flux term is provided with respect the horizontal area of the grid cell (rA). To calculate the total heat flux due to ocean-ice sheet interaction in a model grid cell, sum the ICFhtFlx and SHIhtFlx terms.
comments_2: This diagnostic is only nonzero for grid cells below ice shelves.

name: SIaaflux
long_name: Heat flux required to close the ocean heat budget due to the temperature difference between melt/freeze sea-ice (which occurs at 0 degC) and the ocean
comments_1: Note: excludes the latent heat of fusion.
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN HEAT BUDGET > HEAT FLUX

name: SIarea
long_name: Sea-ice concentration
comments_1: Fraction of ocean grid cell covered with sea-ice, 0 <= SIarea <= 1.
comments_2: Defined using CF Standard Name Table v73: 'Area fraction' is the fraction of a grid cell's horizontal area that has some characteristic of interest. It is evaluated as the area of interest divided by the grid cell area. It may be expressed as a fraction, a percentage, or any other dimensionless representation of a fraction. Sea ice area fraction is area of the sea surface occupied by sea ice. It is also called 'sea ice concentration'. 'Sea ice' means all ice floating in the sea which has formed from freezing sea water and precipitation, rather than by other processes such as calving of land ice to form icebergs. https://cfconventions.org/Data/cf-standard-names/73/build/cf-standard-name-table.html
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > CRYOSPHERE > SEA ICE > SEA ICE CONCENTRATION > ICE FRACTION, EARTH SCIENCE > CRYOSPHERE > SEA ICE > SEA ICE CONCENTRATION
internal note: 1 is the CF convention's unit for dimensionless quantities

name: SIatmFW
long_name: Net freshwater flux into the open ocean, sea-ice, and snow
comments_1: Net freshwater flux into the combined liquid ocean, sea-ice, and snow reservoirs from the atmosphere and runoff.
comments_2: Note: freshwater fluxes BETWEEN the liquid ocean and sea-ice or snow reservoirs do not contribute to SIatmFW. SIatmFW counts all fluxes to/from the atmosphere that change the TOTAL freshwater stored in the combined liquid ocean, sea-ice, and snow reservoirs.
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN CIRCULATION > FRESH WATER FLUX

name: SIatmQnt
long_name: Net heat flux to the atmosphere across open water and sea-ice or snow surfaces
comments_1: The area-weighted sum of heat fluxes across the open water fraction of the grid cell and the sea-ice covered fraction of the grid cell.
comments_2: Note: nonzero SIatmQnt may not be associated with a change in ocean potential temperature due to sea-ice growth or melting. To calculate total ocean heat content changes use the variable TFLUX which also accounts for changing ocean mass (e.g. oceFWflx).
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN HEAT BUDGET > HEAT FLUX
internal note: positive almost everywhere in winter hemisphere (heat loss) and negative almost everywhere in summer hemisphere (heat gain)

name: sIceLoad
long_name: Average sea-ice and snow mass per unit area
comments_1: Total mass of sea-ice and snow in a model grid cell averaged over model grid cell area
comments_2: Note: sIceLoad is used to correct model sea level anomaly, ETAN, to calculate dynamic sea surface height, SSH, and sea surface height without the inverted barometer (IB correction), SSHNOIBC. In the model, sea-ice is treated as floating above the sea level with ETAN tracing the location of the ocean-ice interface. Consequently, sea-ice growth in the model lowers ETAN and sea-ice melting raises ETAN. Dynamic sea surface height is obtained by correcting ETAN by the weight of ice and snow directly above following Archimedes’ principle.
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > CRYOSPHERE > SEA ICE > ICE DEPTH/THICKNESS, EARTH SCIENCE > CRYOSPHERE > SEA ICE > SNOW DEPTH

name: SIFao
long_name: Heat flux out of the uppermost model grid cell to the atmosphere due to exchanges across the open-ocean fraction of a grid cell associated with sea-ice thermodynamic growth/melt
comments_1: Note: this term does not include shortwave radiative fluxes penetrating below the uppermost model grid cell because those fluxes do not contribute to sea-ice growth/melt.
comments_2: This term is scaled by SIgamma (0 <= SIgamma <= 1) when necessary to ensure sea-ice volume does not become negative when melting (see SIgamma and SIgmacnt).
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN HEAT BUDGET > HEAT FLUX

name: SIFFlood
long_name: Mean sea-ice thickness change due to snow flooding
comments_1: When the weight of snow is sufficient to depress the snow/sea-ice interface below the waterline then a quantity of snow is converted to sea-ice to restore the snow/sea-ice interface to the waterline, a process known as snow flooding. SIFFlood is the time rate of change of mean sea-ice thickness, SIheff, due to snow flooding. See SIheff.
comments_2: SIFlood should be >=0 because snow flooding only creates sea-ice from snow.
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > CRYOSPHERE > SEA ICE > ICE DEPTH/THICKNESS

name: SIFianet
long_name: Net heat flux out of sea-ice surface associated with sea-ice thermodynamic growth/melt
comments_1: Under energy balance conditions at the sea-ice surface, there is no heat flux convergence and therefore no surface melting because the sum of upward heat fluxes out of the sea-ice is exactly offset by the upward (conductive) heat flux from below. When the energy balance condition is not met, surface melting occurs.
comments_2: This term is scaled by SIgamma (0 <= SIgamma <= 1) when necessary to ensure sea-ice volume does not become negative when melting (see SIgamma and SIgmacnt).
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN HEAT BUDGET > HEAT FLUX

name: SIFionet
long_name: Net heat flux at the sea-ice bottom associated with sea-ice thermodynamic growth/melt
comments_1: Under energy balance conditions at the sea-ice bottom, there is no heat flux convergence and therefore no bottom melting because the upward heat flux into the sea-ice from the ocean is exactly offset by the upward (conductive) heat flux through the ice. When the energy balance condition is not met, bottom melting occurs.
comments_2: This term is scaled by SIgamma (0 <= SIgamma <= 1) when necessary to ensure sea-ice volume does not become negative when melting (see SIgamma and SIgmacnt).
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN HEAT BUDGET > HEAT FLUX

name: SIFmi
long_name: Heat flux from the uppermost ocean model grid cell to sea-ice
comments_1: This term represents the heat flux from the uppermost ocean model grid cell to sea-ice. It is positive when there is a heat flux from the ocean to the sea-ice which increases sea-ice thickness and negative when there is a heat flux from the sea-ice to the ocean which decreases sea-ice thickness.
comments_2: This term is scaled by SIgamma (0 <= SIgamma <= 1) when necessary to ensure sea-ice volume does not become negative when melting (see SIgamma and SIgmacnt).
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN HEAT BUDGET > HEAT FLUX

name: SIfwThru
long_name: Precipitation through sea-ice
comments_1: Precipitation over sea-ice covered regions reaching ocean through sea-ice
comments_2: Note: Precipitation over sea-ice covered regions that directly reaches ocean through the sea-ice. It is not due to melt of sea-ice/snow.
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN CIRCULATION > FRESH WATER FLUX

name: SIgamma
long_name: A scaling factor between 0 and 1 that is applied to sea-ice heat flux terms to ensure sea-ice volume does not become negative when melting.
comments_1: SIgamma becomes <1 when the net heat fluxes into sea-ice applied over one model time step would melt more sea-ice volume than is actually present. During periods of thermodynamic sea-ice growth, SIgamma =1. It generally only becomes < 1 during the last moments of thermodynamic melt when the sea-ice volume becomes very small.
comments_2: An associated counter, SIgmacnt, increments whenever SIgamma < 1.

name: SIgmacnt
long_name: A counter that increments at each model time step when SIgamma < 1
comments_1: SIgamma < 1 if the net heat fluxes into sea-ice applied over one model time step would melt more sea-ice volume than is actually present (see SIgamma).

name: SIheff
long_name: Area-averaged sea-ice thickness
comments_1: Sea-ice thickness averaged over the entire model grid cell, including open water where sea-ice thickness is zero
comments_2: Note: actual sea-ice thickness over the sea-ice covered fraction of a grid cell is SIheff/SIarea
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > CRYOSPHERE > SEA ICE > ICE DEPTH/THICKNESS

name: SIhsnow
long_name: Area-averaged snow thickness
comments_1: Snow thickness averaged over the entire model grid cell, including open water where snow thickness is zero
comments_2: Note: snow thickness over the ICE-COVERED fraction of the grid cell is SIhsnow/SIarea
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > CRYOSPHERE > SEA ICE > SNOW DEPTH

name: SIsnAccR
long_name: Area-averaged snow accumulation rate over sea-ice
comments_1: Snow accumulation rate averaged over the entire model grid cell, including open water where snow accumulation is zero
comments_2: Note: snow accumulation rate over the ICE-COVERED fraction of the grid cell is SIsnAccR/SIarea

name: SIsnmltS
long_name: Area-averaged snow melt rate over sea-ice
comments_1: Snow melt rate averaged over the entire model grid cell, including open water where snow melt is zero
comments_2: Note: snow melt rate over the ICE-COVERED fraction of the grid cell is SIsnmltS/SIarea

name: SIuice
long_name: Sea-ice velocity in the model +x direction
comments_1: Horizontal sea-ice velocity in the model +x direction at the 'u' face of the tracer cell
comments_2: Note: in the Arakawa-C grid, horizontal velocities are staggered relative to the tracer cells. Positive SIuice(i_g,j) corresponds to +x fluxes through the 'u' face of the tracer cell at (i,j,k=0). Also, the model +x direction does not necessarily correspond to the geographical east-west direction because the x and y axes of the model's curvilinear lat-lon-cap (llc) grid have arbitrary orientations which vary within and across tiles.
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > CRYOSPHERE > SEA ICE > SEA ICE MOTION

name: SIvice
long_name: Sea-ice velocity in the model +y direction
comments_1: Horizontal sea-ice velocity in the model +y direction at the 'v' face of the tracer cell
comments_2: Note: in the Arakawa-C grid, horizontal velocities are staggered relative to the tracer cells. Positive SIvice(i,j_g) corresponds to +y fluxes through the 'v' face of the tracer cell at (i,j,k=0). Also, the model +y direction does not necessarily correspond to the geographical north-south direction because the x and y axes of the model's curvilinear lat-lon-cap (llc) grid have arbitrary orientations which vary within and across tiles.
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > CRYOSPHERE > SEA ICE > SEA ICE MOTION

name: SSH
long_name: Dynamic sea surface height
comments_1: Also known as ocean dynamic sea level.  The local height of the sea surface above the geoid plus a correction due to atmosphere pressure loading, the inverse barometer (IB) correction. SSH is suitable for comparisons with altimetry sea surface height data products that apply the IB correction
comments_2: Note: SSH is calculated by correcting model sea level anomaly ETAN for three effects: a) global mean steric sea level changes related to density changes in the Boussinesq volume-conserving model (Greatbatch correction, see sterGloH), b) the inverted barometer (IB) effect (see SSHIBC) and c) sea level displacement due to sea-ice and snow pressure loading (see sIceLoad). SSH can be compared with the similarly-named SSH variable in previous ECCO products that did not include atmospheric pressure loading (e.g., Version 4 Release 3). Use the field SSHNOIBC for comparisons with altimetry sea surface height data products that do NOT apply the IB correction.
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > SEA SURFACE TOPOGRAPHY > SEA SURFACE HEIGHT

name: SSHIBC
long_name: Inverted barometer (IB) correction to sea surface height
comments_1: The static displacement of the sea surface due to atmosphere pressure loading, known as the inverse barometer (IB) correction. The IB correction is used to calculate dynamic sea surface height (SSH) from sea surface height (SSHNOIBC). SSH = SSHNOIBC - SSHIBC.
comments_2: Note: Use SSH for model-data comparisons with altimetry data products that DO apply the IB correction and SSHNOIBC for comparisons with altimetry data products that do NOT apply the IB correction.
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > SEA SURFACE TOPOGRAPHY > SEA SURFACE HEIGHT

name: SSHNOIBC
long_name: Sea surface height relative to the geoid
comments_1: The local height of the sea surface relative to the geoid. SSHNOIBC does not include the inverted barometer (IB) correction. SSHNOIBC is suitable for comparisons with altimetry sea surface height data products that do NOT apply the inverse barometer (IB) correction
comments_2: Note: SSHNOIBC is calculated by correcting model sea level anomaly ETAN for two effects: a) global mean steric sea level changes related to density changes in the Boussinesq volume-conserving model (Greatbatch correction, see sterGloH), b) sea level displacement due to sea-ice and snow pressure loading (see sIceLoad). The model is forced with atmospheric pressure loading. SSHNOIBC does not correct for the static part of the effect of atmosphere pressure loading on sea surface height (the so-called inverse barometer (IB) correction). Use SSH for comparisons with altimetry data products that DO apply the IB correction.
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > SEA SURFACE TOPOGRAPHY > SEA SURFACE HEIGHT

name: TFLUX
long_name: Rate of change of ocean heat content per m2 accounting for mass fluxes
comments_1: The rate of change of ocean heat content due to heat fluxes across the liquid surface and the addition or removal of mass.
comments_2: Note: the global area integral of TFLUX and geothermal flux (geothermalFlux.bin) matches the time-derivative of ocean heat content (J/s). Unlike oceQnet, TFLUX includes the contribution to the ocean heat content from changing ocean mass (e.g. from oceFWflx).
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN HEAT BUDGET > HEAT FLUX
internal note: positive almost everywhere in summer hemisphere (heat gain) and negative almost everywhere in winter hemisphere (heat loss)

name: THETA
long_name: Potential temperature
comments_1: Sea water potential temperature is the temperature a parcel of sea water would have if moved adiabatically to sea level pressure.
comments_2: Note: the equation of state is a modified UNESCO formula by Jackett and McDougall (1995), which uses the model variable potential temperature as input assuming a horizontally and temporally constant pressure of $p_0=-g ho_{0} z$.
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN TEMPERATURE > POTENTIAL TEMPERATURE

name: TOTUTEND
long_name: U momentum total tendency
comments_1: U momentum tendency from all contributions. TOTUTEND = Um_Cori + (Um_Advec - Um_Cori) + Um_Diss + Um_Ext + Um_dPhiX + AB_gU + Um_ImplD.
comments_2: Note: Um_Advec includes the Um_Cori term, which is why it is added and subtracted in the TOTUTEND calculation. Also, the MITgcm diagnostic package provides TOTUTEND in units of 'm s-1 day-1'. We divide by 86400 to make the units m s-2.

name: TOTVTEND
long_name: V momentum total tendency
comments_1: V momentum tendency from all contributions. TOTVTEND = Vm_Cori + (Vm_Advec - Vm_Cori) + Vm_Diss + Vm_Ext + Vm_dPhiY + AB_gV + Vm_ImplD.
comments_2: Note: Vm_Advec includes the Vm_Cori term, which is why it is added and subtracted in the TOTVTEND calculation. Also, the MITgcm diagnostic package provides TOTVTEND in units of 'm s-1 day-1'. We divide by 86400 to convert the units to m s-2.

name: UBotDrag
long_name: U momentum tendency from bottom drag
comments_1: Component of the u momentum explicit dissipation term, Um_Diss = Um_Diss2 + Um_Diss4 + UBotDrag + USidDrag + UShIDrag.
comments_2: Note: the model +x direction does not necessarily correspond to the geographical east-west direction because the x and y axes of the model's curvilinear lat-lon-cap (llc) grid have arbitrary orientations which vary within and across tiles.

name: Um_Advec
long_name: U momentum tendency from advection
comments_1: Um_Advec is the sum of several u momentum advection terms: Um_Advec = Um_Cori + Um_AdvZ3 + Um_AdvRe + Um_dKEdx.
comments_2: Note: the model +x direction does not necessarily correspond to the geographical east-west direction because the x and y axes of the model's curvilinear lat-lon-cap (llc) grid have arbitrary orientations which vary within and across tiles.

name: Um_AdvRe
long_name: U momentum tendency from vertical advection (explicit part)

name: Um_AdvZ3

comments_1: Component of the u momentum advection term, Um_Advec = Um_Cori + Um_AdvZ3 + Um_AdvRe + Um_dKEdx.

comments_1: Um_Diss is the sum of several u momentum explicit dissipation terms: Um_Diss = Um_Diss2 + Um_Diss4 + UBotDrag + USidDrag + UShIDrag.
comments_2: Note: the model +x direction does not necessarily correspond to the geographical east-west direction because the x and y axes of the model's curvilinear lat-lon-cap (llc) grid have arbitrary orientations which vary within and across tiles.

comments_2: Note: the model +x direction does not necessarily correspond to the geographical east-west direction because the x and y axes of the model's curvilinear lat-lon-cap (llc) grid have arbitrary orientations which vary within and across tiles.

name: UShIDrag

comments_1: Component of the u momentum explicit dissipation term, Um_Diss = Um_Diss2 + Um_Diss4 + UBotDrag + USidDrag + UShIDrag.

name: UVEL
long_name: Horizontal velocity in the model +x direction
comments_1: Horizontal velocity in the model +x direction at the 'u' face of the tracer cell.
comments_2: Note: in the Arakawa-C grid, horizontal velocities are staggered relative to the tracer cells. Positive UVEL(i_g,j,k) corresponds to +x fluxes through the 'u' face of the tracer cell at (i,j,k). Do NOT use UVEL for volume flux calculations because the model's grid cell thicknesses vary with time (z* coordinates); use UVELMASS instead. Also, the model +x direction does not necessarily correspond to the geographical east-west direction because the x and y axes of the model's curvilinear lat-lon-cap (llc) grid have arbitrary orientations which vary within and across tiles. See EVEL and NVEL for zonal and meridional velocity.
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN CIRCULATION, EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN CIRCULATION > OCEAN CURRENTS
internal note: INCLUDE HFACW FOR VOLUME MEAN CALCULATIONS

name: UVELMASS
long_name: Horizontal velocity in the model +x direction per unit area of the grid cell 'u' face
comments_1: Horizontal velocity in the model +x direction averaged over the time-varying area of the tracer grid cell 'u' face. Accounts for partial cells (hFacW < 1) and for time-varying grid cell thickness (z* coordinate system). The volume flux in +x = UVELMASS * drF * dyG.
comments_2: Note: in the Arakawa-C grid, horizontal velocities are staggered relative to the tracer cells. Positive UVELMASS(i,j,k) corresponds to +x fluxes through the 'u' face of the tracer cell at (i,j,k). UVELMASS can be used for volume flux calculations because it accounts for the grid cell thicknesses variations in the +x direction (hFacW) with time (z* coordinate system). Also, the model +x direction does not necessarily correspond to the geographical east-west direction because the x and y axes of the model's curvilinear lat-lon-cap (llc) grid have arbitrary orientations which vary within and across tiles. See VVELMASS and WVELMASS
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN CIRCULATION, EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN CIRCULATION > OCEAN CURRENTS

name: UVELSTAR
long_name: Gent-McWilliams velocity in the model +x direction scaled by time-varying grid cell thickness
comments_1: Gent-McWilliams horizontal velocity in the model +x direction at the 'u' face of the tracer cell.
comments_2: Note: UVELSTAR is not a model diagnostic but is calculated offline: UVELSTAR = -d/dz GM_PsiX. In the Arakawa-C grid, horizontal velocities are staggered relative to the tracer cells. Positive UVELSTAR(i_g,j,k) corresponds to +x tracer fluxes through the 'u' face of the tracer cell at (i,j,k). Also, the model +x direction does not necessarily correspond to the geographical east-west direction because the x and y axes of the model's curvilinear lat-lon-cap (llc) grid have arbitrary orientations which vary within and across tiles. See EVELSTAR and NVELSTAR.

name: VBotDrag
long_name: V momentum tendency from bottom drag
comments_1: Component of the v momentum explicit dissipation term, Vm_Diss = Vm_Diss2 + Vm_Diss4 + VBotDrag + VSidDrag + VShIDrag.
comments_2: Note: the model +y direction does not necessarily correspond to the geographical north-south direction because the x and y axes of the model's curvilinear lat-lon-cap (llc) grid have arbitrary orientations which vary within and across tiles.

name: VISrI_Um
long_name: Vertical flux of u momentum from implicit vertical viscosity
comments_1: Do not use the vertical divergence of VISrI_Um to close the momentum budget because one needs to divide the vertical divergence by the grid cell volume, and the grid cell volume changes with time in the model's z* coordinate system. Instead, use the term Um_ImplD which provides the vertical divergence of VISrI_Um divided by the time-varying grid cell volume.
comments_2: Both VISrI Um(k=1) and VISrI Um(k=Nr+1) should be zero; note that the former is hardcoded in impldiff.F and the latter is not included in the output.

name: VISrI_Vm
long_name: Vertical flux of v momentum from implicit vertical viscosity
comments_1: Do not use the vertical divergence of VISrI_Vm to close the momentum budget because one needs to divide the divergence by the grid cell volume, and the grid cell volume changes with time in the model's z* coordinate system. Instead, use the term Vm_ImplD which provides the vertical divergence of VISrI_Vm divided by the time-varying grid cell volume.
comments_2: Both VISrI Vm(k=1) and VISrI Vm(k=Nr+1) should be zero; note that the former is hardcoded in impldiff.F and the latter is not included in the output.

name: WVEL
long_name: Vertical velocity
comments_1: Vertical velocity in the model +z direction at the top 'w' face of the tracer cell.
comments_2: Note: in the Arakawa-C grid, vertical velocities are staggered relative to the tracer cells. Positive WVEL(i,j,k_l) corresponds to upward +z motion through the top 'w' face of the tracer cell at (i,j,k). WVEL is identical to WVELMASS.
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN CIRCULATION, EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN CIRCULATION > OCEAN CURRENTS
internal note: wvel is the same as wvel mass. wvel, uvel, and vvel are grouped in the 'ocean velocity' dataset. wvelmass, uvelmass, and vvelmass are grouped in the 'ocean volume fluxes' dataset.

name: WVELMASS
long_name: Vertical velocity in the model +z direction per unit area of the grid cell 'w' face
comments_1: Vertical velocity in the model +z direction at the top 'w' face of the tracer cell. Volume flux in +z = WVELMASS drA.
comments_2: Note: in the Arakawa-C grid, vertical velocities are staggered relative to the tracer cells. Positive WVELMASS(i,j,k) corresponds to upward +z motion through the top 'w' face of the tracer cell at (i,j,k). Unlike UVELMASS and VVELMASS, WVELMASS is not scaled by a time-varying open water fraction because the open water fraction of the 'w' face is always 1, thus WVELMASS is identical to WVEL.
GCMD_keywords: EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN CIRCULATION, EARTH SCIENCE > OCEANS > OCEAN CIRCULATION > OCEAN CURRENTS
internal note: INCLUDE DEPTH SO PEOPLE KNOW WHERE BOTTOM IS?

name: WVELSTAR

comments_2: Note: in the Arakawa-C grid, vertical velocities are staggered relative to the tracer cells. Positive WVELSTAR(i,j,k_l) corresponds to upward +z motion through the top 'w' face of the tracer cell at (i,j,k).
